timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Call of Jeremiah

sections.header: Verses 1–12 — The Drought: "Our Backslidings Are Many"

heb.paragraph: Jeremiah calls God "the hope (miqweh) of Israel" (v.8) — but the word miqweh also means "reservoir" or "pool of water." In the context of a drought oracle, the double meaning is pointed: God is both the hope and the water supply that Israel has abandoned. The drought is both literal and theological — the land dries up because the people have left their source.

refs.note: The covenant curses include drought: "The sky over your head will be bronze, the ground beneath you iron." The drought fulfills the covenant penalty.
Elijah's drought was a prophetic sign-act of similar magnitude — God withholding rain as judgment.
Amos describes selective drought as divine discipline: rain on one city, not another. Jeremiah's drought is universal and final.

notes.note: The drought is comprehensive: nobles, farmers, and even wild animals suffer. The doe abandoning her fawn is a particularly haunting image — maternal instinct overcome by environmental catastrophe.

Jeremiah's intercessory prayer acknowledges sin ("our backslidings are many") while appealing to God's name: "Why should you be like a stranger in the land?" The prophet uses God's reputation as leverage for mercy.

God rejects the intercession: the people "love to wander" and have not restrained their feet. Neither fasting, burnt offering, nor grain offering will be accepted. The means of atonement have been exhausted.

Calvin admires the prayer's rhetorical skill: Jeremiah appeals not to Israel's merit but to God's commitment to his own name. "Even when we have no argument from our own worthiness, we can always argue from God's glory."
The word ʾădāmâ ("ground/soil") appears with hāttâ ("is cracked") — the agricultural disaster is described in terms that recall the curse on the ground in Genesis 3:17.
God as "hope of Israel" and "its savior in times of distress" — the titles are ironic in context, since God is both the one they appeal to and the one who sent the drought.

Lundbom identifies this as a "drought liturgy" — a formalized prayer-and-response structure used in communal worship during natural disasters. The liturgy fails because God refuses the prayer, subverting the expected resolution.
Brueggemann reads Jeremiah's prayer as a model of "desperate honesty" — the prophet admits guilt while still insisting that God act. The prayer refuses both cheap repentance and passive fatalism.

hist.context: Chapter 14 describes a devastating drought — one of the primary covenant curses (Deut 28:23–24). The drought affects every level of creation: nobles send servants for water and find nothing (v.3), farmers cover their heads in despair (v.4), even the doe abandons her newborn fawn (v.5). Jeremiah intercedes with a penitential prayer (vv.7–9), but God responds with a third prohibition against intercession (v.11, cf. 7:16, 11:14).

heb.paragraph: The false prophets prophesy šeqer — the same word that characterizes the scribes (8:8), the peace oracle (6:14), and the temple chant (7:4). Šeqer is the signature sin of Jeremiah: a systemic falsehood that pervades every institution. God explicitly disowns these prophets: "I did not send them or appoint them or speak to them" (v.14).

refs.note: The test of a true prophet: if the word does not come true, the prophet has spoken presumptuously. The false prophets fail this test definitively.
Peter warns of false prophets who "secretly introduce destructive heresies" — the NT continuation of Jeremiah's concern.

notes.note: Jeremiah pleads on behalf of the people, blaming the false prophets. God responds: "The prophets are prophesying lies in my name. I have not sent them." Both prophets and people share responsibility.

The punishment fits the crime: false prophets who denied sword and famine will die by sword and famine. Their audiences will share the same fate — those cast into the streets of Jerusalem with no one to bury them.

The closing prayer is among Jeremiah's most eloquent: "Do you despise Zion? Have you rejected Judah?" The appeal to covenant ("we acknowledge our wickedness"), to God's throne, and to creation theology ("Can any of the worthless idols bring rain?") marshals every available theological argument.
Calvin notes that false prophets often claim divine authorization: "The most dangerous lies are those spoken in God's name, because they carry apparent authority."
Calvin commends the closing prayer's theology: "It is you alone, LORD our God. Therefore we put our hope in you." When all else fails, the only argument left is God's own character.
The four terms describing false prophecy — "false visions, divinations, idolatries, and the delusions of their own minds" — cover the full range of unauthorized revelation.
The appeal to God as rain-giver (hammaṭṭîrîm) is particularly poignant in a drought context. Can Baal bring rain? No — only the LORD controls the heavens.

Lundbom identifies the prophet-vs-prophet conflict as the institutional crisis at the heart of Jeremiah. When true and false prophecy use the same vocabulary and claim the same authority, the people have no reliable way to distinguish them except by waiting for outcomes.

Brueggemann calls this prayer "covenant theology in extremis" — Jeremiah uses every category available: confession of sin, appeal to God's character, creation theology, covenant memory. The prayer is a last-ditch attempt to find leverage with a God who has closed the door.

hist.context: Jeremiah's defense — "the prophets are telling them, 'You will not see the sword or suffer famine'" (v.13) — shifts blame to the false prophets. God accepts none of it: the prophets are false, but the people are complicit in choosing to believe comfortable lies. The false prophets will die by the very sword and famine they denied. The chapter closes with another intercessory prayer (vv.19–22) that rehearses covenant language and creation theology.

rows.text: The Drought: Land, People, and Animals

rows.label: vv.19–22
rows.text: Final Prayer: Hope in the Creator Alone

lit.note: Chapter 14 alternates between communal lament and divine response, with the false prophets at the structural center. The drought is both covenant curse and theological crisis.

themes.note: Prophecy peaks at 9 alongside Judgment — the false prophets and the drought form a crisis of prophetic authority. The rejected prayers underscore the finality of divine judgment.

hebtext.note: Jeremiah calls God "the hope (miqweh) of Israel" (v.8) — but the word miqweh also means "reservoir" or "pool of water." In the context of a drought oracle, the double meaning is pointed: God is both the hope and the water supply that Israel has abandoned. The drought is both literal and theological — the land dries up because the people have left their source.

The false prophets prophesy šeqer — the same word that characterizes the scribes (8:8), the peace oracle (6:14), and the temple chant (7:4). Šeqer is the signature sin of Jeremiah: a systemic falsehood that pervades every institution. God explicitly disowns these prophets: "I did not send them or appoint them or speak to them" (v.14).

trans.title: Comparing translations in Drought and False Prophets

translations.text: Why are you like a man taken by surprise, like a warrior powerless to save? You are among us, Lord, and we bear your name; do not forsake us!
Why should you be like a man confused, like a mighty warrior who cannot save? Yet you, O LORD, are in the midst of us, and we are called by your name; do not leave us.”
Wild donkeys stand on the barren heights and pant like jackals; their eyes fail for lack of food.”
The wild donkeys stand on the bare heights; they pant for air like jackals; their eyes fail because there is no vegetation.

Emphasises historical-critical, literary, or text-critical analysis.
Reads Drought and False Prophets within the canonical framework of fulfilled prophecy and covenant theology.
Represented by Calvin, MacArthur, and the evangelical tradition.
Both readings engage the text seriously; they differ primarily in their hermeneutical starting points and assumptions about authorship and historical context.

thread.text: The covenant curses include drought: "The sky over your head will be bronze, the ground beneath you iron." The drought fulfills the covenant penalty.
Elijah's drought was a prophetic sign-act of similar magnitude — God withholding rain as judgment.
Amos describes selective drought as divine discipline: rain on one city, not another. Jeremiah's drought is universal and final.
The test of a true prophet: if the word does not come true, the prophet has spoken presumptuously. The false prophets fail this test definitively.
Peter warns of false prophets who "secretly introduce destructive heresies" — the NT continuation of Jeremiah's concern.

coaching.tip: The drought oracle interweaves environmental disaster with spiritual crisis. Jeremiah refuses to separate creation's suffering from Israel's sin. The land mourns because the people have broken covenant. This ecological theology — where human faithlessness has environmental consequences — runs from Genesis 3 (cursed ground) through Romans 8:22 (creation groaning). Nature and morality are entangled.

'The prophets are prophesying lies in my name. I have not sent them' (v. 14). Jeremiah introduces the theme of true versus false prophecy that dominates chapters 14–29. The false prophets say 'peace'; Jeremiah says 'sword.' Both claim divine authority. The test is Deuteronomy 18:22: does the prediction come true? Jeremiah stakes his credibility on the coming disaster.
